title: Syncope on exertion with a murmur

chart.oneLiner: 72M presents after passing out while climbing stairs; he also reports months of exertional breathlessness and chest tightness.

opening: I blacked out going up the stairs. Lately I've been getting winded and my chest gets tight when I push myself.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 72-year-old man, calm now, concerned. Good historian.

HPI: Syncope occurred DURING exertion (climbing stairs) — a high-risk feature. Over the past few months: exertional dyspnea and exertional chest tightness/angina, plus reduced exercise tolerance (the classic triad). Quick recovery after the faint, no confusion. NO clear prodrome of warmth/nausea. NO palpitations.
PMH: hypertension, hyperlipidemia. MEDS: amlodipine, atorvastatin. SOCIAL: former smoker.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: well-appearing at rest.
- Vitals: BP 118/86 (narrow pulse pressure), HR 78, RR 16, 36.8°C, SpO2 97% RA.
- Cardiac: harsh CRESCENDO-DECRESCENDO systolic ejection murmur, loudest at the right upper sternal border, RADIATING to the carotids; soft/diminished S2; the carotid upstroke is delayed and weak (pulsus parvus et tardus) if specifically examined.
- Lungs: clear. Neuro: no focal deficit.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Emphasize the EXERTIONAL nature of the syncope only if asked when/during what it happened.
- The murmur, radiation, soft S2, and delayed carotid upstroke are revealed only if the heart/carotids are examined.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.Echo: Echocardiogram: severe aortic stenosis (small valve area, elevated gradient) with left ventricular hypertrophy and preserved EF.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Left ventricular hypertrophy with possible repolarization (strain) changes; sinus rhythm; no acute ST elevation.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Aortic stenosis causing exertional syncope (leading & CANT-MISS,9). Arrhythmia (CANT-MISS,4). Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (core,3). Vasovagal (less likely — exertional) (core,2). Pulmonary embolism (CANT-MISS,2). Connects exertional syncope + systolic murmur radiating to carotids to outflow obstruction (core,5).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Aortic stenosis causing exertional syncope. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Arrhythmia; Pulmonary embolism. Also reasonable: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy; Vasovagal (less likely — exertional).

coreActions.item: Connects exertional syncope + systolic murmur radiating to carotids to outflow obstruction

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: EKG (CANT-MISS,4). Echocardiogram — the key diagnostic test (CANT-MISS,6). Telemetry (2). Troponin (1). BNP (1). CXR (1). PENALTY: discharging an exertional syncope with a concerning murmur without an echo (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; Echocardiogram — the key diagnostic test.

penalties.item: discharging an exertional syncope with a concerning murmur without an echo

contraindicatedTests: discharging an exertional syncope with a concerning murmur without an echo
orderSensitiveActions: discharging an exertional syncope with a concerning murmur without an echo

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = LVH with possible strain; sinus rhythm; no acute ischemia. CREDIT: rate & rhythm (4); identifies LVH (high voltages) (6); notes strain/repolarization changes and no acute STEMI (3); systematic read (2). PENALTY: over-calling acute ischemia (-3).
keyFindings: notes strain/repolarization changes and no acute STEMI
criticalFindings: Left ventricular hypertrophy with possible repolarization (strain) changes; sinus rhythm; no acute ST elevation.
steps.idealAnswer: Left ventricular hypertrophy with possible repolarization (strain) changes; sinus rhythm; no acute ST elevation.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Left ventricular hypertrophy with possible repolarization (strain) changes; sinus rhythm; no acute ST elevation.

coreActions.item: notes strain/repolarization changes and no acute STEMI

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = clear lungs, possible cardiomegaly/valve calcification. CREDIT: identifies a normal or mildly enlarged heart (5); excludes acute pulmonary pathology (3); notes possible valve calcification (2). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-2).
keyFindings: identifies a normal or mildly enlarged heart
criticalFindings: Possible cardiomegaly / aortic valve calcification; lungs clear.
steps.idealAnswer: Possible cardiomegaly / aortic valve calcification; lungs clear.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Possible cardiomegaly / aortic valve calcification; lungs clear.

coreActions.item: identifies a normal or mildly enlarged heart

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Given the echo showing severe AS, what is your diagnosis and why is exertional syncope significant?
steps.rubric: Severe aortic stenosis with exertional syncope (6). Recognizes that the onset of syncope/angina/heart failure in severe AS signals a poor prognosis and the need for valve intervention (4).

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH valvular disease (AS): refer for aortic valve replacement – AS is a surgical disease (CANT-MISS,7); echocardiogram to confirm severity/EF (5); cardiology/structural consult (4); avoid aggressive preload reduction – nitrates/diuretics (4); cautious BP management, maintain narrow volume range (3). PENALTY: nitrates/aggressive diuresis/vasodilators causing hypotension in severe AS (-3); medical management alone for symptomatic severe AS (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Symptomatic severe AS (syncope) is a surgical disease – refer for aortic valve replacement (SAVR vs TAVI by surgical risk). Echocardiography to confirm severity and EF. Cardiology/structural consult. Manage hypertension cautiously (start low, go slow) and keep within a narrow volume range – avoid aggressive preload reduction (nitrates/diuretics) and vasodilators. Avoid hypotension.

coreActions.item: Echocardiogram to confirm severity and EF

penalties.item: Vasodilators/diuretics causing hypotension in severe AS

optionalActions: Echocardiogram to confirm severity and EF
unsafeActions: Vasodilators/diuretics causing hypotension in severe AS

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 72M presents after passing out while climbing stairs; he also reports months of exertional breathlessness and chest tightness. The diagnosis is Aortic stenosis, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Cardiac: harsh CRESCENDO-DECRESCENDO systolic ejection murmur, loudest at the right upper sternal border, RADIATING to the carotids; soft/diminished S2; the car

reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Aortic stenosis while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Aortic stenosis and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
commonPitfalls: Vasodilators/diuretics causing hypotension in severe AS
